summary: Trayvon Martin, a 17-year-old African-American teenager from Miami Gardens, was fatally shot by neighborhood-watch volunteer George Zimmerman on February 26, 2012, in The Retreat at Twin Lakes gated community in Sanford, Florida. The incident, captured by surveillance cameras, became a national controversy that catalyzed the Black Lives Matter movement.
locationLabel: The Retreat at Twin Lakes, Sanford, Florida

claims.object: The Retreat at Twin Lakes, Sanford, Florida

historicalContext: This record anchors the specific location of the encounter, which is critical for understanding the racial dynamics and community tensions in Sanford, Florida, during the early 2010s. While the legal outcome (Zimmerman's acquittal) is a separate legal proceeding, the documented facts of the shooting itself serve as a primary case study for discussions on racial profiling and police violence.

summary: Sean Bell, 23, was killed by five plainclothes NYPD officers who fired 50 bullets at his silver Nissan Altima on November 25, 2006, in Jamaica, Queens. The shooting occurred after Bell and friends left a bachelor party at Club Kalua, a topless bar located at 143-08 94th Avenue. Bell was to be married the following day, and the incident resulted in the wounding of two other friends, Joseph.

summary: This record identifies the specific location of Breonna Taylor's death: Apartment 4 at 3003 Springfield Drive #4 in the St. Anthony Gardens complex in Louisville, Kentucky. Police entered the apartment at approximately 12:40 a.m. on March 13, 2020, pursuant to a drug-trafficking warrant. The address is confirmed through search warrant materials and grand-jury transcriptions, distinguishing it.
locationLabel: St. Anthony Gardens, 3003 Springfield Drive #4, Louisville, Kentucky
historicalContext: This location serves as the physical anchor for the Breonna Taylor case, illustrating the intersection of law enforcement operations, the 'no-knock' policy debate, and the policing of drug markets in Louisville. The specific apartment building and unit number are critical for understanding the spatial dynamics of the raid and the subsequent national reckoning regarding police accountability.

summary: Eric Garner died on July 17, 2014, after a plainclothes NYPD officer placed him in a chokehold during an arrest at 202 Bay Street in Tompkinsville, Staten Island. Garner was allegedly caught selling untaxed cigarettes at the location. His recorded last words, 'I can't breathe,' became a national protest refrain and a lasting marker of police use-of-force accountability.

claims.object: after a chokehold during an arrest

claims.predicate: was approached by a plainclothes police officer
claims.object: in front of a beauty supply store

historicalContext: This record documents a pivotal moment in the history of police use-of-force and racial justice, occurring outside a beauty supply store in Staten Island. The incident, where Garner was arrested for allegedly selling untaxed cigarettes, led to the viral spread of his 'I can't breathe' statement and subsequent nationwide protests demanding accountability.

summary: On February 4, 1999, Amadou Diallo, a 23-year-old Guinean immigrant, was shot 19 times by four NYPD officers at his building in the Bronx. Contemporary reporting confirms the officers fired 41 shots at an unarmed man in the vestibule of his apartment building, an event that became a pivotal case in late 1990s police accountability.

claims.citationLabel: Officers in Bronx fire 41 shots and an unarmed man is killed
Officers in Bronx fire 41 shots and an unarmed man is killed

historicalContext: This record matters as a primary example of police use-of-force controversies in the late 1990s, occurring within the context of the NYPD's Street Crime Unit and the broader societal tensions surrounding racial profiling and unarmed fatalities in New York City.

summary: Jordan Neely, 30, died on May 1, 2023, after being placed in a chokehold by a fellow passenger on a northbound F train. The incident occurred after Neely boarded at Second Avenue and the train stopped at the Broadway-Lafayette Street station in Manhattan. His death resulted from neck compression and sparked national debates regarding subway safety and the use of force against Black New Yorkers.

claims.citationLabel: Jordan Neely died from compression of the neck after a chokehold by a fellow passenger on a New York City subway train.

historicalContext: This record documents a specific instance of extrajudicial death in the New York City subway system, occurring shortly after the death of Eric Garner and amidst heightened scrutiny of police interactions with Black citizens. The location at Broadway-Lafayette Street serves as a critical public landmark where the initial altercation and subsequent emergency response unfolded, highlighting the intersection of public transit safety and civil rights.

summary: Michael Griffith, 23, was killed in Howard Beach, Queens, on December 20, 1986. After leaving New Park Pizzeria on Cross Bay Boulevard, he was chased by a white mob and fatally struck by a car while fleeing onto the Belt Parkway.
locationLabel: Cross Bay Boulevard near 157th Avenue / New Park Pizzeria, Howard Beach, Queens
historicalContext: This incident is a documented case of racial violence in 1980s New York City, occurring in the same geographic corridor as other high-profile racial killings. It serves as a specific example of the broader pattern of mob violence against Black men in the late 1980s, distinct from the 1990s Trayvon Martin case.

summary: Emmett Louis Till, a 14-year-old African American boy from Chicago, was abducted, tortured, and lynched in Mississippi in August 1955. His body was recovered near Graball Landing on the Tallahatchie River, an area now part of the Emmett Till and Mamie Till-Mobley National Monument. His murder became a catalyst for the modern civil rights movement, highlighted by his open-casket funeral and his.
locationLabel: Graball Landing, Tallahatchie River, near Glendora, Mississippi

summary: Clementa C. Pinckney was a pastor at Mother Emanuel AME Church in Charleston, South Carolina, and a state senator. On June 17, 2015, she was among nine African American members of the church who were killed during a white-supremacist attack at the church while attending a Bible study.
locationLabel: Emanuel AME Church (Mother Emanuel), 110 Calhoun Street, Charleston
historicalContext: This record documents a specific victim of the June 17, 2015 Charleston church shooting, an event that resulted in the deaths of nine congregants during a Bible study at 110 Calhoun Street. The incident is historically significant as a targeted attack on a Black religious community and is often cited in discussions regarding racial violence and the Emanuel Nine.

summary: Cynthia Graham Hurd was a member of the Emanuel African Methodist Episcopal Church in Charleston, South Carolina, who was killed on June 17, 2015, during a Bible study. She is remembered as one of the nine victims, known collectively as the Emanuel Nine, who were murdered at the church located at 110 Calhoun Street.
locationLabel: Emanuel AME Church (Mother Emanuel), 110 Calhoun Street, Charleston

summary: Myra Thompson was an African American Bible study teacher at Mother Emanuel AME Church in Charleston, South Carolina.
